aliases: 焼きビーフカレーライス

japanese: 焼きビーフカレーライス

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general curry rice food product class; no specific Japanese yaki beef curry rice entry exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FJP covers rice dishes broadly; no specific Japanese beef curry rice sub-code exists. Approximate match used at rice-dish category level.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No defensible SNOMED CT code identified for Japanese beef curry rice as a specific food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of yaki beef curry rice served in a Singapore hawker or food court context typically weighs approximately 350 g, comprising a mound of white rice topped with grilled beef slices and Japanese-style curry sauce with vegetables.

nutrient_source.title: 553D Yaki Beef Curry Rice — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for '553D Yaki Beef Curry Rice(D)' (cr_id G00010977). All per-100g macro and micronutrient values are sourced from this canonical HPB record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is not reported in the HPB record; value estimated from USDA FoodData Central beef curry rice analogue (USDA SR Legacy #21340) and documented accordingly. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and several vitamins are not reported in the HPB source and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; white rice (boiled) GI reference; Japanese curry rice analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Japanese yaki beef curry rice. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for white rice (boiled, GI ~64–72) as the dominant carbohydrate component, moderated downward slightly by the presence of dietary fibre (3.7 g/100g), protein (6.5 g/100g), and fat (4.21 g/100g) from the beef and curry sauce, which slow gastric emptying. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 × 88.2) / 100 = 57.3, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 25.19 × 3.5 = 88.2 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100g; a 350 g serving delivers approximately 809 mg sodium, representing ~35% of the 2,300 mg daily limit, partially offset by potassium 327 mg/100g (1,145 mg per serving).
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced-sodium curry roux, avoid adding soy sauce or salt at the table, and limit portion size to manage cumulative daily sodium intake within DASH diet targets.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: White rice base with estimated GI 65 and glycemic load ~57 per standard serving; carbohydrate 25.19 g/100g delivers ~88 g per 350 g plate, posing significant postprandial glucose elevation risk.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should substitute brown rice or cauliflower rice to reduce glycaemic impact, strictly control portion size to no more than 150–200 g of the dish, and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat estimated at 1.6 g/100g from grilled beef and curry roux; sodium 231 mg/100g; dietary fibre 3.7 g/100g is a positive factor supporting LDL reduction.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should choose lean cuts of beef, request reduced-sodium curry roux, and rely on the dish's dietary fibre content (3.7 g/100g) as a partial cardioprotective benefit while keeping total portion size moderate to limit saturated fat and sodium load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red beef contributes arachidonic acid, a precursor to pro-inflammatory eicosanoids that amplify TNF-alpha and IL-6 cytokine activity; processed curry roux may contain trace trans fats.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should consider substituting chicken or fish for the beef component, request roux brands with no partially hydrogenated oils, and be aware that potato in the curry is a nightshade vegetable to which some RA patients report individual sensitivity.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Yaki Beef Curry Rice (焼きビーフカレーライス) is a Japanese-style rice dish consisting of steamed white rice served alongside or topped with grilled (yaki) beef slices and a rich Japanese curry sauce. The curry sauce is typically prepared from a commercial Japanese curry roux block containing wheat flour, vegetable oils, curry spices, and seasoning, combined with onion, potato, and carrot simmered until tender. The grilled beef adds a slightly charred, savoury depth that distinguishes this preparation from standard simmered beef curry rice. The dish is widely available in Singapore food courts and hawker centres under the '553D' menu code, reflecting its institutional food service origin. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides 164 kcal, 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat, 25.19 g carbohydrate, 3.7 g dietary fibre, and 231 mg sodium. The relatively high dietary fibre content (3.7 g/100g) is notable for a rice-based dish and is attributable to the vegetable components of the curry. The estimated glycaemic index is 65 (medium), but the glycaemic load per standard 350 g serving is high (~57), making portion control critical for blood glucose management. The dish is suitable for individuals with hypertension at moderate portions given the potassium-to-sodium ratio, but is a concern for type 2 diabetes patients due to the white rice base and high per-serving carbohydrate load. Cardiovascular risk is moderate, driven by saturated fat from beef and roux. The dish is not suitable for rheumatoid arthritis patients without modification due to red meat's pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid content.

confidence_reason: Core macro and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry for this exact dish (cr_id G00010977, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy beef curry rice analogue and documented. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database white rice reference with documented analogous reasoning. All mandatory Japanese-language naming requirements satisfied.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these values are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this dish (G00010977), and no sufficiently close approved-source analogue was available to provide defensible estimates for these specific micronutrients. 2. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 in the HPB source; this is accepted as confirmed zero per the HPB canonical record — confirmed zero per HPB Foodo G00010977. 3. saturated_fat_g estimated at 1.6 g/100g from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy beef curry rice analogue (closest approved-source match); this is an adapted value and not directly reported in the HPB record. 4. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database white rice (boiled) reference, moderated by the dish's fibre, protein, and fat content; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish exists in any approved source. 5. Halal status is unconfirmed — the dish contains beef and curry roux of unspecified certification; consumers requiring halal-certified food should verify with the specific food court operator. 6. The dish name prefix '553D' is an institutional menu code used in Singapore food court and hawker centre settings and does not carry nutritional significance.
